---
title: "Volatility Targeting"
date: "2026-06-10"
categories: [algorithmic-trading, volatility]
description: "How volatility targeting strategies dynamically scale position sizes to maintain a constant level of portfolio risk."
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: '@*'
---

Volatility targeting is a position sizing technique that adjusts exposure inversely to realised volatility, keeping portfolio risk approximately constant through time.

## The Core Idea

Rather than holding a fixed number of shares or a fixed notional position, a volatility-targeted strategy scales its position so that the expected portfolio volatility equals a target level $\sigma^*$:

$$w_t = \frac{\sigma^*}{\hat{\sigma}_t}$$

where $\hat{\sigma}_t$ is an estimate of current volatility, typically a rolling standard deviation of recent returns.

## Why It Works

Volatility is mean-reverting and persistent. When markets are calm, vol targeting increases exposure; when markets are turbulent, it reduces it. This creates a natural mechanism for cutting risk during drawdowns — often before stop-losses would trigger.

## Practical Considerations

The choice of lookback window for $\hat{\sigma}_t$ matters significantly. Short windows are more responsive but noisier; long windows are more stable but lag regime changes. Transaction costs must also be weighed against the benefits of frequent rebalancing.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<hr class="revision-divider">
<details>
<summary>Revision History</summary>

- June 2026 — First published

</details>
